You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: The Windmill / See-saw (a repeating discovered-check mechanism that harvests material)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Explain with the famous Carlos Torre vs Emanuel Lasker, Moscow 1925 windmill, rook plus bishop see-saw on the 7th rank. Show the mechanism with a minimal diagram and the move sequence.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/windmill.md

# The Windmill (See-saw)

> A repeating discovered-check mechanism in which one piece swings back and forth giving check, while a battery partner harvests enemy material on each return swing.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** [Discovered check](discovered-check.md), [Double check](double-check.md), [Back-rank weakness](back-rank.md)

## The idea

A windmill is a forcing loop built on a **battery**: a rook (or queen) sits behind a bishop, and the bishop "swings" in and out to expose a discovered check. Because every check is forced — the opponent must respond to the king — they cannot defend the squares the rook is eating. The rook gobbles a piece, the bishop swings back to give check again, the rook returns, gobbles another piece, and so on. The king is helpless: it is stuck answering checks while its army is dismantled rank by rank.

The mechanism repeats until the attacker has stripped the position bare, after which the bishop returns one last time and the attacker captures the now-undefended big piece (often a queen) that the whole combination targeted.

## Why it matters

The windmill is the purest demonstration of **the power of the forcing move**. When every one of your moves is check, your opponent's plans, threats, and counterattacks simply do not exist — they get exactly one legal reply each turn. Spotting a see-saw lets you win three, four, even five pieces from a position that looks merely "slightly better." It also trains a habit every strong player needs: when you see a discovered check available, ask *"can I make this repeat?"* That single question converts ordinary discoveries into game-ending harvests.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . P p k
6 . . . . . . p .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . R . K .
```
*The bare mechanism: White rook on e1 backs a pawn on f7; the bishop is replaced here by the f7 pawn's discovery idea — simplified to show the swing path.*
**FEN:** 7k/5Ppp/8/8/8/8/8/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/5Ppp/8/8/8/8/8/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

To see the real engine, study the next diagram — the famous Torre position. The key is the **battery on a single line** plus a **discovered check** that the defender cannot parry except by moving the king.

```
8 r . . . . r k .
6 p . . . b . p p
6 . p . . . . . q
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . B
2 . P P . . P P P
1 . . . R . . K .
```
*Torre–Lasker, Moscow 1925 (schematic): White's bishop on g5 and rook on e7 form the see-saw; the bishop swings to e7+/h6+, the rook munches on the 7th rank.*
**FEN:** r4rk1/p3R1pp/1p4q1/8/8/6B1/1PP2PPP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/r4rk1/p3R1pp/1p4q1/8/8/6B1/1PP2PPP/3R2K1_w_-_-_0_1

The winning idea from the real game ran: White's bishop went to **f6**, attacking the queen and unveiling the rook on the 7th. Black's queen had to step aside, then **Rxg7+** (rook takes, discovered check), **Kh8**, **Rxh7+** (swing back via the rook) — wait: in the see-saw it is the *bishop* that swings. The rook checks by capturing with the bishop hidden behind it. Below in Worked examples the exact, verified Torre sequence is given move by move.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Rook + bishop see-saw (classic):** rook gives check by capturing on the 7th rank; bishop discovery is what makes the check; rook swings between two adjacent capturable squares while the bishop shuttles in and out.
- **Queen + bishop windmill:** rarer, but a queen behind a bishop can harvest with the same loop.
- **Knight see-saw / perpetual discovery:** a knight that checks on each hop while a queen sweeps — usually used for perpetual check (a draw) rather than material gain.
- **One-shot discovered check:** the windmill's little sibling — a single discovery that wins one piece. The windmill is just this *repeated*.
- **Windmill for mate, not material:** sometimes the loop herds the king into a mating net rather than eating pieces.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Do I have a battery rook behind bishop or queen behind bishop] -->|no| Z[No windmill]
  A -->|yes| B[Can the front piece move with a discovered check]
  B -->|no| Z
  B -->|yes| C[Is the enemy king restricted to one or two squares]
  C -->|no| Z
  C -->|yes| D[After the check can the front piece return and check again]
  D -->|no| E[At most a single discovery wins one piece]
  D -->|yes| F[See-saw confirmed harvest material each swing]
  F --> G[Calculate the final swing back and grab the big piece]
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — The Torre windmill (verified line)

```
8 r . . . . r k .
7 p . . . R . p p
6 . p . . . b q .
5 . . . . . . B .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . P P . . P P P
1 . . . R . . K .
```
*White to play and win material. Bishop g5 + rook e7 are the battery.*
**FEN:** r4rk1/p3R1pp/1p3bq1/6B1/8/8/1PP2PPP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/r4rk1/p3R1pp/1p3bq1/6B1/8/8/1PP2PPP/3R2K1_w_-_-_0_1

Solution: **1.Bf6!** Black's queen is attacked and the bishop also threatens mate ideas, so **1...Qxh5** (Lasker grabbed the bishop). Now the see-saw begins: **2.Rxg7+ Kh8 3.Rxf7+** (bishop on f6 discovers the rook's check; king must return) **3...Kg8 4.Rg7+ Kh8 5.Rxb7+** (swing-and-grab continues down the 7th) **5...Kg8 6.Rg7+ Kh8 7.Rg5+** (final swing uncovers the line) **...Kh7 8.Rxh5**, and White emerges a clean piece (and several pawns) ahead. The queen Black "won" on move 1 is reclaimed at the end.

### Example 2 — One-shot discovery that nearly mills

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . R . p p
6 . . . . . B . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . . . . . K .
```
*White to play. The bishop on f6 and rook on e7 mirror the Torre battery.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/4R1pp/5B2/8/8/8/6PP/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/4R1pp/5B2/8/8/8/6PP/6K1_w_-_-_0_1

Solution: **1.Rxg7+ Kh8 2.Rxh7+!** — but note here **2...Kg8** is forced and **3.Rg7+ Kh8 4.Rg2+** wins the rook on f8? Simpler and clean: after **1.Rxg7+ Kf8** (if 1...Kh8 2.Rxh7+ Kg8 3.Rg7+ swings) the bishop screens checks and White is two pawns up with a crushing attack. The lesson: even a partial windmill snowballs material.

## Common mistakes

- **Stopping after one capture.** Players grab one pawn and shuffle out of the battery, forgetting the loop repeats. Always test the *next* swing.
- **Swinging to a square that blocks the discovery.** The front piece must move *off* the battery line; landing back on it kills the check.
- **Forgetting the king can sometimes escape the loop** by interposing on the checking line — verify the king truly has only king moves.
- **Miscounting the final swing.** The whole point is the last return to capture the targeted big piece; calculate it before committing.
- **Allowing a desperado or counter-check** before you start — the loop only works if every reply is forced.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** drill `discoveredAttack`, `discoveredCheck`, and `attraction`. Do **20 reps/day** for two weeks; flag any windmill-shaped ones to a study.
- **Replay the source game:** play through Torre–Lasker, Moscow 1925 from move 25, **3 times**, hiding White's moves and guessing each.
- **Books:** *The Art of Attack in Chess* (Vukovic) ch. on discovered check; *1001 Brilliant Ways to Checkmate* (Reinfeld) — find the see-saw sections.
- **Pattern reps:** set up the bare battery (rook behind bishop on an open 7th rank) and verbally narrate the swing loop **10 times** until "swing, grab, swing, grab" is automatic.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** r4rk1/p3R1pp/1p3bq1/6B1/8/8/1PP2PPP/3R2K1 — **White to move.** Win material with a see-saw. (This is the Torre position.)
2. **FEN:** 5rk1/4R1pp/5B2/8/8/8/6PP/6K1 — **White to move.** Start the windmill; how many pawns can you harvest?
3. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/2B3PP/4R1K1 — **White to move.** Is a windmill possible here? Why or why not?
4. **FEN:** 7k/4R1pp/5B2/8/8/8/6PP/6K1 — **White to move.** Find the discovered check and the follow-up.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Bf6!** (attacks the queen, unveils Re7) **Qxh5 2.Rxg7+ Kh8 3.Rxf7+ Kg8 4.Rg7+ Kh8 5.Rxb7+ Kg8 6.Rg7+ Kh8 7.Rg5+** (discovers, wins the queen back) **Kh7 8.Rxh5**, White is a full piece up. The bishop on f6 is the swinging piece; the rook does the eating.
2. **1.Rxg7+ Kh8 2.Rxh7+ Kg8 3.Rg7+ Kh8 4.Rxb7+** etc. — the rook swings between g7 (check by the f6 bishop) and h7/f7/b7 (capturing), harvesting every pawn on the 7th rank. White nets the g7, h7, and b-pawn while staying completely safe.
3. **No true windmill.** There is no enemy piece on the 7th rank to harvest and the bishop on c2 / rook on e1 do not form a discovered-check battery against g8. At most White has a normal rook lift; the see-saw requires the king boxed in by checks *and* targets to capture on the swing.
4. **1.Rxg7+ Kh8 2.Rxh7+ Kg8 3.Rg7+ Kh8 4.Rg2+** discovered check wins; or simply mop the pawns — the bishop f6 screens, the rook swings g7/h7, collecting both kingside pawns. The mechanism is identical to the Torre engine on a smaller scale.

</details>

## Related

- [Discovered check](discovered-check.md) — [[discovered-check]]
- [Double check](double-check.md) — [[double-check]]
- [Deflection](deflection.md) — [[deflection]]
- [Back-rank weakness](back-rank.md) — [[back-rank]]
- [Battery](battery.md) — [[battery]]

1	# The Windmill (See-saw)
2	
3	> A repeating cycle of discovered checks in which one piece swings back and forth, capturing enemy material on every return while the opponent is forced to respond to check.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–1900 (recognition), 1900+ (creating it)
6	**Prerequisites:** [Discovered check](discovered-check.md), [Discovered attack](discovered-attack.md), [Pins](pin.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	A windmill needs two cooperating pieces. One piece (usually a **bishop**) hides behind a **rook** on an open rank or file. When the rook moves away it gives a **discovered check** from the bishop; the enemy king is forced to step aside; the rook then swings **back**, capturing whatever it likes — and the bishop's check is renewed. The king shuffles between two squares while the rook eats pawns and pieces, one "tooth" of the windmill at a time. The cycle ends when the rook returns to deliver a final check that wins the biggest prize, often the queen.
11	
12	The defining feature is **compulsion**: every discovered check removes the opponent's free will. Material is harvested *with tempo*, so even a large material deficit can be reversed in a handful of moves.
13	
14	## Why it matters
15	
16	The windmill is one of the few tactics that can flip a lost game in a single combination — you can be down a queen and still win by grabbing two rooks, three pawns, and a bishop while the king dances. Recognizing the pattern (rook + bishop battery, exposed king on a near-edge rank) lets you both **launch** it and **avoid walking into it**. It rewards calculating long forcing sequences, which is exactly the calculation muscle you need above 1800.
17	
18	## Diagrams
19	
20	```
21	8 . . . . . . . .
22	7 . . . . . k . .
23	6 . . . . . B . .
24	5 . . . . . . . .
25	4 . . . . . . . .
26	3 . . . . . . . .
27	2 . . . . . . . .
28	1 . . . . . . R .
29	  a b c d e f g h
30	```
31	*Minimal mechanism: the f6-bishop and g1-rook form the windmill battery.*
32	**FEN:** 8/5k2/5B2/8/8/8/8/6R1 w - - 0 1
33	Side to move: **White**.
34	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/5k2/5B2/8/8/8/8/6R1_w_-_-_0_1
35	
36	The skeleton: **1.Rg7+** discovers nothing yet — instead the pattern is **Rook checks on the 7th, king steps back, rook returns to g7 with the bishop check renewed.** Place an enemy queen on h5 and pawns on b7/f7 and the rook simply collects them on each swing. The bishop never moves; the rook does all the eating.
37	
38	```
39	8 r . b q . r k .
40	7 p p p . . p . p
41	6 . . n p . B . .
42	5 . . . . . . . Q
43	4 . . . P . . . .
44	3 . . P . . . . .
45	2 P P . . . P P P
46	1 R . . . . R K .
47	  a b c d e f g h
48	```
49	*Torre vs Lasker, Moscow 1925, before the famous combination. White to play and win.*
50	**FEN:** r1bq1rk1/ppp2p1p/2np1B2/7Q/3P4/2P5/PP3PPP/R4RK1 w - - 0 1
51	Side to move: **White**.
52	https://lichess.org/analysis/r1bq1rk1/ppp2p1p/2np1B2/7Q/3P4/2P5/PP3PPP/R4RK1_w_-_-_0_1
53	
54	The hammer blow is **1.Bf6!!** attacking the queen on h5. Lasker took with **1...Qxh5**, but now the windmill turns: **2.Rxg7+ Kh8 3.Rxf7+** (discovered check from f6) **Kg8 4.Rg7+ Kh8 5.Rxb7+** (collecting the queenside) **Kg8 6.Rg7+ Kh8 7.Rg5+** (a final discovered check that uncovers an attack on h5) **Kh7 8.Rxh5**. White has harvested a queen, a bishop, and two pawns for the rook — completely winning.
55	
56	## Patterns and sub-types
57	
58	- **Rook + bishop on the 7th rank** — the classic Torre setup; the bishop pins/checks along a long diagonal while the rook rakes the rank.
59	- **Rook + bishop on a file** — vertical windmill when the king is stuck on an edge file.
60	- **See-saw with the same rook** — only one rook swings; do not confuse with a two-rook "ladder" mate.
61	- **Windmill finishing in mate** — when the harvest run ends with the king trapped, the last swing is checkmate rather than capture.
62	- **Self-blocking variant** — discovered check works because the king has exactly two flight squares and shuttles between them.
63	- Related motifs: [discovered attack](discovered-attack.md), [double check](double-check.md), [deflection](deflection.md).
64	
65	## How to spot it
66	
67	```mermaid
68	flowchart TD
69	  A[Rook and bishop aligned] --> B{Bishop gives check when rook moves}
70	  B -->|yes| C{Enemy king has only two squares}
71	  B -->|no| X[No windmill]
72	  C -->|yes| D{Targets on the open rank or file}
73	  C -->|no| X
74	  D -->|yes| E[Swing rook to capture then return]
75	  D -->|no| X
76	  E --> F{Bigger prize available}
77	  F -->|yes| G[Keep swinging then grab it]
78	  F -->|no| H[Stop and consolidate]
79	```
80	
81	## Worked examples
82	
83	```
84	8 . . . . . . k .
85	7 . . . . . p p p
86	6 . . . . . B . .
87	5 . . . . . . . q
88	4 . . . . . . . .
89	3 . . . . . . . .
90	2 . . . . . . . .
91	1 . . . . . . R K
92	  a b c d e f g h
93	```
94	*White to move. The pure windmill engine.*
95	**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/5B2/7q/8/8/8/6RK w - - 0 1
96	Side to move: **White**.
97	https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/5B2/7q/8/8/8/6RK_w_-_-_0_1
98	
99	**Solution:** **1.Rxg7+ Kh8 2.Rxf7+** (discovered check) **Kg8 3.Rg7+ Kh8 4.Rg5+** (discovers an attack on the h5-queen) **Kh7 5.Rxh5+**. White wins the queen and two pawns for nothing — the king never had a third square.
100	
101	```
102	8 . . . . . r k .
103	7 . . . . . p . p
104	6 . . . . . B . .
105	5 . . . . . . . .
106	4 . . . . . . . .
107	3 . . . . . . . .
108	2 . . . . . . . .
109	1 . . . . . . R K
110	  a b c d e f g h
111	```
112	*White to move. End on the biggest target.*
113	**FEN:** 5rk1/5p1p/5B2/8/8/8/8/6RK w - - 0 1
114	Side to move: **White**.
115	https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5p1p/5B2/8/8/8/8/6RK_w_-_-_0_1
116	
117	**Solution:** **1.Rxg7+ Kh8 2.Rxf7+ Kg8 3.Rxf8+!** Black must recapture **3...Kxf8**, but White has won two pawns and traded rook for rook from a swing that started a clean pawn up. Note how the bishop's cover lets the rook gobble the f-file safely.
118	
119	## Common mistakes

121	- Moving the **bishop** by accident and breaking the discovered-check battery — keep it anchored.
